title: Iris Static Site Generator
content: Iris is a lightweight static site generator designed for building clean, fast documentation — no bloat, no complex configuration, just structured content that works. Whether you're documenting an internal tool, an open-source library, or a personal project, Iris gives you a focused writing environment and a polished result out of the box. Get started in minutes. Clone the repository, drop your Markdown files into docs/, and you're done. No build pipeline to configure, no dependencies to wrestle with. Built to be yours. Iris doesn't lock you in. The structure is intentionally minimal — tweak the styles, swap the icons, replace the branding. Everything is where you'd expect it to be. Open source. Iris is publicly available on GitHub. Found a bug? Have an idea? Open an issue or submit a pull request — contributions are welcome.

title: Getting Started
content: Iris SSG is a lightweight static site generator built for documentation. This page walks you through cloning the repository and getting your first documentation site running locally. Prerequisites. No build tools or package managers are required. Iris SSG runs entirely in the browser — all you need is a static file server to avoid CORS issues when loading Markdown files via fetch. A simple option is the Live Server extension for VS Code, or Python's built-in HTTP server: python3 -m http.server 8080. Then open http://localhost:8080 in your browser. Installation. Clone the official repository and navigate into the project directory: git clone https://github.com/stepan-vana/iris-ssg.git. That's it. No npm install, no build step, no configuration files to touch. Directory Structure. Your documentation files go into docs/ as standard Markdown files. The assets/ directory contains the runtime, styles, and icons — all freely modifiable. docs.json is the allowlist of permitted Markdown paths. search-index.json is the full-text search index. Your First Page. Create a new Markdown file in docs/. Register the page in index.html by adding a sidebar link with a data-md attribute. Add the path to assets/docs.json so the page is permitted to load and included in search. Open index.html via your local server — your page loads instantly, no rebuild required. Page Metadata. Iris SSG supports optional metadata tags at the top of any Markdown file. They render a date label, author block with GitHub avatar, read time, and action buttons above the page title. Supported tags: date, author with pp:: GitHub username prefix, read_time, copy_raw_file, view_as_md. Next Steps. Replace assets/logo/iris_ssg.svg with your own logo. Update the GitHub link in index.html to point to your repository. Adjust the color scheme by editing the CSS variables at the top of assets/styles.css. Add entries to assets/search-index.json to make new pages fully searchable.

title: Developer Guide
content: This guide is the authoritative reference for extending, customizing, and maintaining a documentation site built with Iris SSG. It describes the actual runtime behavior of the application as implemented in assets/script.js, assets/styles.css, and index.html. Initialization. On load, initApp() fetches assets/docs.json and populates the ALLOWED_DOCS array, calls initSearchIndex() to load assets/search-index.json into the global searchIndex array, then calls loadMD to render the default page docs/home.md. To change the default page, update the path passed to loadMD at the bottom of initApp() in script.js. Adding Pages. Create a .md file in docs/. Register the path in docs.json — this is the security allowlist, any path not present is blocked and loadMD returns false. Add a sidebar link in index.html using a data-md attribute. Any element with data-md anywhere in the document triggers loadMD on click. Update search-index.json with a new entry containing path, anchor, title, subtitle, and content fields. Markdown and Meta Syntax. parseMetaSyntax() processes custom inline tags before HTML is injected into the DOM. Meta tags are processed after pre blocks are temporarily extracted and replaced with placeholders, preventing code block examples from being parsed as directives. Supported tags: date renders a meta-date div; author with pp::username fetches a GitHub avatar from github.com/<username>.png, falls back to initials if unavailable; multiple authors supported by chaining pp:: pairs; read_time renders a meta-read-time div with a clock icon; copy_raw_file renders a button that copies the raw Markdown source via navigator.clipboard.writeText(), label switches to Copied! for 1.5 seconds; view_as_md renders a button that opens the raw Markdown in a new tab as a Blob object URL revoked after 10 seconds. Four block-level meta tags render styled callout boxes for highlighting information within page content: note, tip, warning, and danger. Each renders as a div with a class corresponding to its type (meta-note, meta-tip, meta-warning, meta-danger), an icon, and the provided text as its content to draw attention to important caveats, shortcuts, breaking changes, or destructive operations. All meta action buttons are collected and moved into a shared meta-actions-row div after DOM population. Table of Contents. buildTOC() queries all h2 elements after each page load. IDs are assigned via slugify() — lowercase, spaces to hyphens, non-word characters removed. If no h2 headings are found, sidebar-left is hidden. Scroll synchronization uses IntersectionObserver with root margin -10% 0px -80% 0px. To include h3 headings, extend the querySelectorAll selector in buildTOC(). Code Blocks. enhanceCodeBlocks() wraps each pre element in a code-header with a language label and copy button using assets/icons/copy.svg and check.svg. Language is read from the language-* class set by marked. hljs.highlight() is used for known languages, hljs.highlightAuto() as fallback. Blocks are guarded by data-done=1 to prevent reprocessing. Syntax Highlighting. updateCodeTheme() runs on load and on prefers-color-scheme change, switching between github.min.css and github-dark.min.css from the Cloudflare CDN by updating the href of the link element with id hljs-light-theme. LaTeX Rendering. If KaTeX renderMathInElement is available, it is called after HTML injection. Supported delimiters: $$...$$ and [...] for display mode, $...$ and (...) for inline. throwOnError is false. Tables. All table elements are wrapped in a div.table-wrapper after render to enable horizontal scrolling on narrow viewports. HTML Sanitization. sanitizeHTML() uses DOMParser and enforces an allowlist of tags: h1-h6, p, a, ul, ol, li, pre, code, blockquote, strong, em, b, s, del, div, span, img, table, thead, tbody, tr, th, td, hr, br, button, i. Allowed attributes: id, class, href, src, alt, title, data-md, data-action, target, style. href and src values starting with javascript: are stripped. Search. search-index.json is a flat array with fields path, anchor, title, subtitle, content. Loaded once into global searchIndex on startup. Search triggers on every input event, ignores queries under 2 characters, lowercases and trims the query, performs case-insensitive substring scan across title, subtitle, content. No stemming, tokenization, ranking, or fuzzy matching. Results appear in index order. Snippets are ~90 characters centered on the first match: 30 characters before and 60 after the match index. Query terms highlighted with mark elements; query is escaped with escapeRegExp before regex construction. Clicking a result calls loadMD, waits 120ms then scrolls to anchor if present. Keyboard shortcut Ctrl+K or Cmd+K focuses the search input. Theming. CSS custom properties in assets/styles.css: --bg page background, --text primary text, --text-muted secondary text, --text-light placeholders, --border dividers, --code-bg code block background, --btn-inverted-bg primary button fill, --btn-inverted-text primary button label, --icon-filter SVG icon filter. Light values: bg #ffffff, text #111111, text-muted #333333, text-light #999999, border #f0f0f0, code-bg #f5f5f5, btn-inverted-bg #111111. Dark values: bg #000000, text #ffffff, border #1f1f23, code-bg #1b1b1b, btn-inverted-bg #ffffff, btn-inverted-text #000000. Dark overrides inside @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark). Fonts. Geist from Google Fonts for body text and UI. JetBrains Mono for code blocks and headers. Both require link preconnect and stylesheet tags in the head of index.html. Icons. Tabler Icons via webfont CDN, any ti-* class usable in HTML or Markdown. Local SVGs in assets/icons/ referenced by filename in script.js — swap by replacing files with same names. Replacing Branding. Navbar logo is assets/logo/iris_ssg.svg. In dark mode it receives brightness(0) invert(1) filter automatically — override with .nav-logo img { filter: none } inside the dark mode media query if logo is multicolor. View on GitHub button in navbar and sidebar — update onclick URL in index.html or remove the button. Mobile Navigation. Sidebar hidden below 768px, toggled by hamburgerBtn which adds open class to sidebar and sidebar-open to body. Both removed on navigation. Sidebar flyout menus .sidebar-flyout are hover-activated on desktop and click-toggled on mobile when the link contains .sidebar-chevron.

title: Contributing
content: Iris SSG is an open-source project and we welcome contributions from anyone. Whether you're fixing a bug, improving the documentation, or proposing a new feature — your input is valued. Submitting a Pull Request. If you have a fix or improvement ready, the best way to contribute is through a pull request on GitHub. 1. Fork the Iris SSG repository 2. Create a new branch for your change 3. Make your changes and commit them with a clear message 4. Open a pull request and describe what you've changed and why. We review all pull requests and aim to respond promptly. There's no contribution too small — typo fixes and documentation improvements are just as welcome as new features. Reporting Bugs & Security Issues. If you come across a bug, a visual glitch, or a security vulnerability, please let us know. General bugs — open an issue on GitHub with a description of the problem, steps to reproduce it, and your browser/OS if relevant. Security vulnerabilities — please do not open a public issue. Instead, reach out to us directly so the issue can be addressed before any public disclosure. The more detail you provide, the faster we can address it. Join the Team. We're building a small open-source community around Iris SSG and are looking for people who want to be involved on an ongoing basis — not just as one-time contributors, but as part of the team. If you're interested in helping shape the direction of the project, reviewing pull requests, or maintaining documentation, get in touch. There's no formal process — just reach out via GitHub and we'll go from there. Code of Conduct. We expect all contributors to engage respectfully and constructively. This is a welcoming project and we intend to keep it that way.
